content:
# Disagreement Log — Joshua Chapter 8

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA

---

### 8:1 — "Do not fear and do not be dismayed" vs. "Do not be afraid, and do not lose heart" (al-tira ve-al-techat)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Do-not fear and-do-not be-dismayed." |
| LOGOS | "Do not fear and do not be dismayed." |
| PNEUMA | "Do not be afraid, and do not lose heart." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Do not fear and do not be dismayed." — PNEUMA renders yare as "be afraid" (acceptable) but renders chatat as "lose heart," an interpretive paraphrase. While chatat can include the sense of losing courage, its primary meaning is "be shattered, be dismayed." EMET and LOGOS both use "dismayed," which is the standard formal equivalent. The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS. The conjunction "and" without a comma follows Hebrew paratactic style.

---

### 8:1 — "the people of war" vs. "the fighting men" (am ha-milchamah)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "all the-people of-the-war" |
| LOGOS | "all the people of war" |
| PNEUMA | "all the fighting men" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "all the people of war" — The Hebrew am ha-milchamah means literally "people of the war." PNEUMA's "fighting men" is interpretive, collapsing am ("people") into "men" and restructuring the construct chain. The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS.

---

### 8:1 — "See" vs. "Look" (re'eh)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "See" |
| LOGOS | "See" |
| PNEUMA | "Look" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "See" — Both are acceptable renderings of re'eh. EMET/LOGOS agree on "See," and it carries a slightly more formal register. The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS.

---

### 8:2 — "her-king" vs. "its king" (le-malkah)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "her-king" (reflecting feminine suffix on Ai) |
| LOGOS | "its king" |
| PNEUMA | "its king" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "its king" — Hebrew Ai is grammatically feminine, and EMET preserves the feminine pronominal suffix. In English, cities are conventionally referred to as "it/its." The consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA for natural English while noting EMET's grammatical precision.

---

### 8:2 — "her-spoil and-her-livestock you-shall-plunder" vs. "its spoil and its livestock you shall plunder" vs. "you may keep the plunder and the livestock" (raq shelalah u-vehemtah tavozu lakhem)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "only her-spoil and-her-livestock you-shall-plunder for-yourselves" |
| LOGOS | "Only its spoil and its livestock you shall plunder for yourselves" |
| PNEUMA | "except this time you may keep the plunder and the livestock for yourselves" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Only its spoil and its livestock you shall plunder for yourselves." — PNEUMA adds "this time" (not in Hebrew), changes the verb from "plunder" (bazaz) to "keep," and restructures the clause order. The Hebrew fronts the objects (spoil and livestock) for emphasis, which EMET/LOGOS preserve. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 8:2 — "Set for yourself an ambush for the city from behind her" vs. "Set an ambush behind the city" (sim lekha orev la-ir me-achareyha)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Set for-yourself an-ambush for-the-city from-behind-her." |
| LOGOS | "Set for yourself an ambush against the city from behind it." |
| PNEUMA | "Set an ambush behind the city." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Set for yourself an ambush against the city from behind it." — PNEUMA omits "for yourself" (lekha), which is significant as a direct personal address to Joshua. PNEUMA also omits the preposition distinguishing "against the city" from the directional "from behind it." The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 8:3 — "And-arose Joshua and-all the-people" vs. "So Joshua arose, and all the people of war" vs. "So Joshua and all the fighting men prepared to march" (va-yaqam Yehoshua ve-khol am ha-milchamah)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-arose Joshua and-all the-people of-the-war to-go-up to-the-Ai." |
| LOGOS | "So Joshua arose, and all the people of war, to go up to Ai." |
| PNEUMA | "So Joshua and all the fighting men prepared to march against Ai." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And Joshua arose, and all the people of war, to go up to Ai." — (a) PNEUMA replaces "arose" (qum) with "prepared to march," a significant paraphrase. (b) PNEUMA uses "fighting men" instead of "people of war" (see v. 1 note). (c) EMET uses "the-Ai" preserving the Hebrew article; the consensus drops it for English convention. (d) The opening conjunction: EMET uses "And" (binding convention); LOGOS uses "So" (interpretive); the consensus uses "And" per binding convention.

---

### 8:3 — "thirty thousand men, mighty men of valor" vs. "of his best warriors" (sheloshim elef ish gibborei ha-chayil)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "thirty thousand men, mighty-men of-valor" |
| LOGOS | "thirty thousand men, mighty men of valor" |
| PNEUMA | "thirty thousand of his best warriors" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "thirty thousand men, mighty men of valor" — PNEUMA collapses the Hebrew appositive construction into "of his best warriors," losing the specific term gibborei ha-chayil ("mighty men of valor"), which is a technical military designation in Hebrew. The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS.

---

### 8:4 — "See, you are lying-in-ambush" vs. "See, you are to lie in ambush" vs. "Listen carefully — you are to set an ambush" (re'u atem orvim)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "See, you [are] lying-in-ambush against the-city" |
| LOGOS | "See, you are to lie in ambush against the city" |
| PNEUMA | "Listen carefully — you are to set an ambush behind the city" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "See, you are to lie in ambush against the city, from behind the city." — PNEUMA renders re'u ("see") as "Listen carefully," which changes the sensory verb entirely. The Hebrew participle orvim ("lying in ambush") describes their assigned role. LOGOS's "are to lie in ambush" captures the instructional force. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 8:4 — "exceedingly" vs. "very far" vs. "very far" (harbeh me'od)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Do-not go-far-away from the-city exceedingly" |
| LOGOS | "Do not go very far from the city" |
| PNEUMA | "Do not go very far from it" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Do not go very far from the city" — EMET's "exceedingly" is hyper-literal for me'od. LOGOS/PNEUMA both use "very far," which is natural English. PNEUMA substitutes "it" for "the city" (min ha-ir); the consensus preserves the explicit noun. Follows LOGOS.

---

### 8:4 — "prepared" vs. "ready" vs. "stay ready" (nekhonim)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "and-be all-of-you prepared" |
| LOGOS | "and all of you be ready" |
| PNEUMA | "All of you, stay ready" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "and all of you be ready" — PNEUMA omits the conjunction "and" and adds "stay." The Hebrew nekhonim means "ready, prepared." Both "prepared" (EMET) and "ready" (LOGOS) work; "ready" is slightly more natural. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 8:9 — "and-they-sat" vs. "and stayed" vs. "took up a position" (va-yeshvu)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "and-they-sat between Bethel and-between the-Ai" |
| LOGOS | "and stayed between Bethel and Ai" |
| PNEUMA | "took up a position between Bethel and Ai" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "and stayed between Bethel and Ai" — The Hebrew yashav means "to sit, dwell, stay." PNEUMA's "took up a position" is an interpretive military paraphrase. EMET preserves the doubled "between...and between" (bein...u-vein), which is standard Hebrew syntax for expressing "between X and Y." The consensus simplifies to English convention "between...and..." following LOGOS.

---

### 8:9 — "from the west of Ai" vs. "on the west side of Ai" vs. "to the west of Ai" (mi-yam la-Ai)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "from-[the]-west of-the-Ai" |
| LOGOS | "on the west side of Ai" |
| PNEUMA | "to the west of Ai" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "on the west side of Ai" — The Hebrew mi-yam means literally "from the sea" (= west, since the Mediterranean is west of Israel). LOGOS's "on the west side" is a clear positional rendering. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 8:11 — "the valley was between him and between the Ai" vs. "with a valley between them and Ai" vs. "There was a valley between them and Ai" (ve-ha-gai beino u-vein ha-Ai)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "and-the-valley [was] between-him and-between the-Ai" |
| LOGOS | "with a valley between them and Ai" |
| PNEUMA | "There was a valley between them and Ai" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And the valley was between them and Ai." — EMET preserves the doubled "between...and between" and the singular "him" (the Hebrew has beino, singular, referring to the camp or Joshua). The consensus uses "them" for English clarity (following LOGOS/PNEUMA) and uses "the valley" with the article (following the Hebrew ha-gai). LOGOS uses "with a valley" (no article, subordinating clause); the consensus makes it an independent clause with the article, closer to the Hebrew syntax.

---

### 8:13 — "its rear" vs. "its rear guard" vs. "the rear guard" (aqevo)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "its-rear" (literally "its heel") |
| LOGOS | "its rear guard" |
| PNEUMA | "the rear guard" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "its rear guard" — The Hebrew aqev means "heel," used metaphorically for the rear portion or rearguard of a military formation. "Rear guard" captures the military sense. LOGOS's possessive "its" links it to the camp. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 8:14 — "And-it-was when-saw the king of Ai" vs. "And it was, when the king of Ai saw it" vs. "When the king of Ai saw this" (va-yehi kir'ot melekh ha-Ai)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-it-was when-saw [the] king of-the-Ai" |
| LOGOS | "And it was, when the king of Ai saw it" |
| PNEUMA | "When the king of Ai saw this" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And it was, when the king of Ai saw it" — PNEUMA omits the opening va-yehi ("And it was"), which serves as a Hebrew temporal marker and narrative pacer. The consensus preserves it following LOGOS.

---

### 8:14 — "the appointed place" vs. "the designated place" (ha-mo'ed)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the-appointed-place" |
| LOGOS | "the appointed place" |
| PNEUMA | "the designated place" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "the appointed place" — Both "appointed" and "designated" work for mo'ed. EMET/LOGOS agree on "appointed," which is the more traditional rendering. The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS.

---

### 8:15 — "And-were-struck Joshua and all Israel" vs. "Joshua and all Israel let themselves be struck" vs. "Joshua and all Israel let themselves be beaten back" (va-yinnag'u Yehoshua ve-khol Yisra'el)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-were-struck Joshua and-all Israel before-them, and-they-fled [by the] way of-the-wilderness." |
| LOGOS | "And Joshua and all Israel let themselves be struck before them and fled by the way of the wilderness." |
| PNEUMA | "Joshua and all Israel let themselves be beaten back and fled toward the wilderness." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And Joshua and all Israel let themselves be struck before them, and they fled by the way of the wilderness." — (a) The Niphal va-yinnag'u has a tolerative sense here — they allowed themselves to appear defeated. EMET's simple passive "were struck" misses this nuance; LOGOS's "let themselves be struck" captures it well. (b) PNEUMA uses "beaten back," which adds directional force not in the Hebrew. (c) PNEUMA omits "And" at the start. (d) PNEUMA uses "toward" for derekh ("way/road"), which is less precise. The consensus follows LOGOS with slight punctuation adjustment.

---

### 8:16 — "to pursue after them" vs. "to pursue them" vs. "to chase them" (lirdof achareihem)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "to-pursue after-them" |
| LOGOS | "to pursue them" |
| PNEUMA | "to chase them" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "to pursue after them" — The Hebrew uses both radaf ("pursue") and acharei ("after"), and EMET preserves both elements. LOGOS drops "after" and PNEUMA substitutes "chase." The consensus preserves the fuller Hebrew construction "pursue after" following EMET.

---

### 8:17 — "And-not was-left a-man" vs. "And there was not a man left" vs. "Not a man was left" (ve-lo nish'ar ish)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-not was-left a-man in-the-Ai and-Bethel who did-not go-out after Israel." |
| LOGOS | "And there was not a man left in Ai or in Bethel who had not gone out after Israel." |
| PNEUMA | "Not a man was left in Ai or Bethel who had not gone out after Israel." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And there was not a man left in Ai or in Bethel who had not gone out after Israel." — PNEUMA omits the opening "And" (binding convention). LOGOS's phrasing is clear and preserves the Hebrew structure. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 8:17 — "And-they-left the-city open" vs. "They left the city wide open" (va-ya'azvu et ha-ir petuchah)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-they-left the-city open" |
| LOGOS | "And they left the city open" |
| PNEUMA | "They left the city wide open" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And they left the city open" — PNEUMA adds "wide" (not in the Hebrew, which simply says petuchah, "open"). The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS.

---

### 8:18 — "into your hand I will give it" vs. "I will give it into your hand" vs. "I am giving it to you" (be-yadekha etenennah)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "into-your-hand I-will-give-it" |
| LOGOS | "I will give it into your hand" |
| PNEUMA | "I am giving it to you" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "I will give it into your hand." — The Hebrew fronts "into your hand" for emphasis. LOGOS restructures for English but preserves all elements. PNEUMA's "I am giving it to you" loses the specific Hebrew idiom "into your hand" (be-yad), which carries covenantal and military significance. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 8:19 — "when he stretched out his hand" vs. "as soon as he stretched out his hand" vs. "The moment he stretched out his hand" (kin'toto yado)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "when-he-stretched-out his-hand" |
| LOGOS | "as soon as he stretched out his hand" |
| PNEUMA | "The moment he stretched out his hand" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "as soon as he stretched out his hand" — The Hebrew infinitive construct kin'toto conveys temporal immediacy. LOGOS's "as soon as" and PNEUMA's "The moment" both capture this. The consensus follows LOGOS for consistency of register.

---

### 8:20 — "and behold" vs. omitted (ve-hinneh)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "and-behold, went-up [the] smoke of-the-city to-the-heavens" |
| LOGOS | "and behold, the smoke of the city ascended to the heavens" |
| PNEUMA | "they saw smoke rising from their city into the sky" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "and behold, the smoke of the city ascended to the heavens" — PNEUMA omits the Hebrew hinneh ("behold"), which marks a moment of sudden perception — the men of Ai turn and see their city burning. This is a dramatic pivot in the narrative. PNEUMA also changes "the heavens" (ha-shamayim) to "the sky" and restructures. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 8:20 — "turned upon the pursuer" vs. "turned back against the pursuers" vs. "turned on their pursuers" (nehpakh el ha-rodef)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "turned upon the-pursuer" |
| LOGOS | "turned back against the pursuers" |
| PNEUMA | "turned on their pursuers" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "turned back against the pursuers" — The Hebrew uses the singular ha-rodef ("the pursuer") collectively. English requires the plural for clarity. LOGOS's "turned back against" best captures the Hebrew nehpakh el (to reverse direction toward). The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 8:22 — "these from this side and these from this side" vs. "some on this side and some on that side" vs. "with Israelites on both sides" (elleh mi-zzeh ve-elleh mi-zzeh)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "these from-this-[side] and-these from-this-[side]" |
| LOGOS | "some on this side and some on that side" |
| PNEUMA | "with Israelites on both sides" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "some on this side and some on that side" — EMET preserves the Hebrew's repetitive structure (mi-zzeh...mi-zzeh) but produces awkward English. PNEUMA summarizes too loosely. LOGOS captures the sense with natural English. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 8:22 — "a survivor or an escapee" vs. "a survivor or a fugitive" vs. "no survivor or fugitive" (sarid u-falit)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "a-survivor or-an-escapee" |
| LOGOS | "a survivor or a fugitive" |
| PNEUMA | "no survivor or fugitive" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "a survivor or a fugitive" — The Hebrew word pair sarid u-falit is a hendiadys meaning "survivor and/or escapee." EMET's "escapee" and LOGOS's "fugitive" both work for palit. "Fugitive" is the more standard English rendering. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 8:23 — "And the king of Ai they seized alive" vs. "But the king of Ai they captured alive" vs. "But the king of Ai they captured alive" (ve-et melekh ha-Ai tafsu chai)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And the-king of-the-Ai they-seized alive" |
| LOGOS | "But the king of Ai they captured alive" |
| PNEUMA | "But the king of Ai they captured alive" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And the king of Ai they captured alive" — The Hebrew conjunction ve- is "and," not "but." LOGOS/PNEUMA render it as "But" to indicate a contrast (the king was not killed). While the adversative sense is valid in context, the consensus preserves "And" per binding convention and uses "captured" (taphas, LOGOS/PNEUMA) rather than "seized" (EMET), as "captured" is the more standard rendering for taking a prisoner alive.

---

### 8:24 — "to kill" vs. "slaying" (la-harog)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "to-kill all [the] inhabitants of-the-Ai" |
| LOGOS | "slaying all the inhabitants of Ai" |
| PNEUMA | "killing all the inhabitants of Ai" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "slaying all the inhabitants of Ai" — All three use forms of "kill/slay." LOGOS's "slaying" provides a slightly more formal register appropriate to the narrative. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 8:25 — "from man and unto woman" vs. "both men and women" vs. "both men and women" (me-ish ve-ad ishah)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "from-man and-unto woman" |
| LOGOS | "both men and women" |
| PNEUMA | "both men and women" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "both men and women" — The Hebrew me-ish ve-ad ishah ("from man and unto woman") is an idiom for totality. EMET's hyper-literal rendering, while faithful, produces unnatural English. LOGOS/PNEUMA both render the idiom naturally. The consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

### 8:27 — "the spoil of the city that" vs. "the spoil of that city" vs. "the plunder of the city" (shelal ha-ir ha-hi)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the-spoil of-the-city that" |
| LOGOS | "the spoil of that city" |
| PNEUMA | "the plunder of the city" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "the spoil of that city" — EMET's word order follows the Hebrew (ha-ir ha-hi, "the city, that one"), but English requires the demonstrative before the noun. PNEUMA omits the demonstrative "that" (ha-hi) entirely and uses "plunder" instead of "spoil." The consensus follows LOGOS, preserving all elements in natural English word order.

---

### 8:28 — "a mound of perpetuity, a desolation" vs. "a mound forever, a desolation" vs. "a permanent ruin, a desolation" (tel olam shemamah)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "a-mound of-perpetuity, a-desolation, unto this day" |
| LOGOS | "a mound forever, a desolation unto this day" |
| PNEUMA | "a permanent ruin, a desolation to this day" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "a mound forever, a desolation unto this day" — PNEUMA's "permanent ruin" loses the specific term tel ("mound/tell"), which has distinct archaeological significance. EMET's "of perpetuity" is stilted. LOGOS's "forever" is natural and accurate for olam. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 8:29 — "corpse" vs. "body" vs. "body" (nivlato)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "his-corpse" |
| LOGOS | "his body" |
| PNEUMA | "the body" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "his body" — The Hebrew nivlah specifically means "corpse/carcass," and EMET's rendering is more precise. However, LOGOS/PNEUMA both use "body," which is standard in English narrative. The consensus follows LOGOS for readability while noting EMET's precision.

---

### 8:29 — "opening of the gate" vs. "entrance of the gate" vs. "entrance of the city gate" (petach sha'ar ha-ir)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "[the] opening of-[the]-gate of-the-city" |
| LOGOS | "the entrance of the gate of the city" |
| PNEUMA | "the entrance of the city gate" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "the entrance of the gate of the city" — PNEUMA restructures the Hebrew construct chain, moving "city" into an attributive position ("city gate"). The Hebrew has three nouns in construct: petach ("opening/entrance") + sha'ar ("gate") + ha-ir ("the city"). LOGOS preserves the full chain. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 8:31 — "scroll of the Torah" vs. "book of the Torah" vs. "book of the law" (sefer torat Mosheh)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "scroll of-[the]-Torah of-Moses" |
| LOGOS | "book of the Torah of Moses" |
| PNEUMA | "book of the law of Moses" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "book of the Torah of Moses" — (a) EMET uses "scroll" for sefer, which while etymologically defensible (sefer can mean scroll), is not standard in English Bible translation. "Book" is the conventional rendering. (b) PNEUMA uses "law" for Torah, while EMET/LOGOS retain "Torah." The consensus retains "Torah" per established SOPHIA convention, consistent with Joshua 1. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 8:31 — "whole stones" vs. "uncut stones" (avanim shelemot)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "whole stones" |
| LOGOS | "whole stones" |
| PNEUMA | "uncut stones" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "whole stones" — The Hebrew shelemot means "whole, complete, intact." PNEUMA's "uncut" is an interpretation (stones are whole because they have not been cut), but the Hebrew word itself means "whole," not "uncut." EMET/LOGOS both render literally. The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS.

---

### 8:31 — "no one had wielded iron" vs. "no iron tool had been used" (lo henif aleihem barzel)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "no-[one] had-wielded iron" |
| LOGOS | "no one has wielded an iron tool" |
| PNEUMA | "no iron tool had been used" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "no one has wielded iron" — The Hebrew henif (Hiphil of nuf, "to wave, wield") takes a human subject; PNEUMA's passive construction ("had been used") obscures the active Hebrew verb. EMET/LOGOS preserve the active voice. LOGOS adds "tool" (not in the Hebrew, which says simply barzel, "iron"). The consensus follows EMET's closer rendering but with LOGOS's grammar: "no one has wielded iron."

---

### 8:32 — "which he wrote" vs. "which he had written" (asher katav)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "which he-wrote before the-sons of Israel" |
| LOGOS | "which he had written before the Sons of Israel" |
| PNEUMA | "which he had written, in the presence of the Sons of Israel" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "which he had written before the Sons of Israel" — The pluperfect ("had written") is appropriate in English context since the writing occurred at that time. PNEUMA's "in the presence of" for lifnei is interpretive; "before" is the standard rendering. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 8:33 — "the priests, the Levites" vs. "the Levitical priests" (ha-kohanim ha-Levi'im)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the-priests, the-Levites" |
| LOGOS | "the Levitical priests" |
| PNEUMA | "the Levitical priests" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "the Levitical priests" — The Hebrew ha-kohanim ha-Levi'im can be parsed as either "the priests, the Levites" (two groups) or "the Levitical priests" (priests of Levitical descent). The Deuteronomic usage (Deut 17:9, 18:1) treats it as one group: priests who are Levites. LOGOS/PNEUMA follow this understanding. The consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

### 8:33 — "the sojourner as well as the native-born" vs. "Foreigners and native-born alike" (ka-ger ka-ezrach)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "as-the-sojourner as-the-native-born" |
| LOGOS | "the sojourner as well as the native-born" |
| PNEUMA | "Foreigners and native-born alike" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "the sojourner as well as the native-born" — (a) PNEUMA renders ger as "Foreigners" (plural, capitalized), which does not preserve the specific legal category of the ger ("sojourner/resident alien"), distinct from nokri ("foreigner"). (b) The Hebrew uses the comparative kaph (ka-...ka-...) meaning "as the...as the...," emphasizing equal standing. LOGOS's "as well as" captures this inclusivity. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 8:33 — "Ark" capitalization
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the-Ark" |
| LOGOS | "the ark" |
| PNEUMA | "the ark" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "the Ark" — Per SOPHIA convention, "Ark" is capitalized when referring to the Ark of the Covenant, consistent with its sacred status. EMET capitalizes; LOGOS/PNEUMA do not. The consensus capitalizes throughout.

---

### 8:34 — "And afterward" vs. "And after this" vs. "After this" (ve-acharei-khen)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And-afterward" |
| LOGOS | "And after this" |
| PNEUMA | "After this" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And afterward" — PNEUMA omits the opening "And" (binding convention). The Hebrew ve-acharei-khen means "and after thus/so" — "afterward" (EMET) is a cleaner rendering than "after this" (LOGOS). The consensus follows EMET.

---

### 8:34 — "the Torah" / "the book of the Torah" vs. "the law" / "the book of the law" (ha-Torah / sefer ha-Torah)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the-Torah" / "scroll of the-Torah" |
| LOGOS | "the Torah" / "the book of the Torah" |
| PNEUMA | "the law" / "the book of the law" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "the Torah" / "the book of the Torah" — Per established SOPHIA convention, Torah is retained untranslated. PNEUMA's "law" narrows the semantic range of Torah (which also means "instruction, teaching"). The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 8:35 — "the sojourner, the one walking in their midst" vs. "the sojourners who walked in their midst" vs. "the foreigners living among them" (ve-ha-ger ha-holekh be-qirbam)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the-sojourner, the-[one]-walking in-their-midst" |
| LOGOS | "the sojourners who walked in their midst" |
| PNEUMA | "the foreigners living among them" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "the sojourners who walked in their midst" — (a) The Hebrew uses the singular ha-ger ("the sojourner") collectively; LOGOS renders it as plural for natural English. (b) PNEUMA uses "foreigners" instead of "sojourners" (see v. 33 note). (c) PNEUMA renders ha-holekh ("the one walking") as "living," which is a valid sense of halakh in context but loses the more concrete image. (d) PNEUMA uses "among them" for be-qirbam; LOGOS uses "in their midst," which is closer to the Hebrew qerev ("midst/interior"). The consensus follows LOGOS.
